exemplar.title:
How does modelling scoring rates as differential equations compare to the
Duckworth-Lewis-Stern method in predicting run chases in rain-affected limited-overs cricket?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How does modelling scoring rates as differential equations compare to the
Duckworth-Lewis-Stern method in predicting run chases in rain-affected limited-overs cricket?

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The research question is explicit, focused and tightly connected to every section of the essay, ensuring a coherent investigative thread.
Explanatory passages on both cricket rules and differentialâequation modelling are accurate, logically sequenced and pitched at an appropriate mathematical audience.
Frequent signposting (âto evaluate their effectivenessâ¦â) keeps the discussion centred on the research question throughout.
weaknesses: A few extended historical digressions (e.g. origins of cricket) marginally distract from the core modelling focus.
Empirical methodology is robust but limited by analysing only two matches and by brief justification of ad-hoc parameter choices (e.g. vertical shifts).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The topic is communicated accurately and effectively, and the methodology is largely complete with clear evidence of informed selection of methods and sources. Minor scope limitations in the empirical data constrain this from the top band, warranting a score of 5.

strengths: Derivations of both exponential and logistic differential equations are thorough and correctly applied to match data.
Key subject-specific terminology (e.g. âWiener process,â âcarrying capacityâ) is generally appropriate and underpins the modelling discourse.
weaknesses: Notation is inconsistently applied (e.g. $R(t)$ vs. scoring rate vs. cumulative runs), causing occasional ambiguity.
Some derivations are treated informally (e.g. parameter proofs) and statistical underpinnings (e.g. justification of resource percentages) are superficial.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates good knowledge of calculus and stochastic concepts but is marred by notation slips and uneven rigor in statistical justification. This places it solidly in the mid-band for knowledge and understanding, meriting a score of 3.

strengths: Research draws on reputable sources (ESPNcricinfo, academic texts) and integrates data into clear tables with manual RÂ² calculations.
Analysis is clearly presented with step-by-step derivations, model fitting and tabulated comparisons against the DLS outputs.
The discussion offers a reasoned argument about model performance and the potential of stochastic extensions, acknowledging key limitations.
weaknesses: Empirical scope is limited to two matches, reducing generalisability and depth of research quality.
Statistical evaluation relies heavily on RÂ² without deeper exploration of residuals, confidence intervals or hypothesis testing.
The ad-hoc vertical shift parameter is introduced without theoretical justification, risking overfitting.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student presents a clear analysis and a reasoned discussion with critical elements, but the limited dataset and relatively shallow statistical treatment constrain this to the mid-band for critical thinking, justifying a score of 4.

strengths: The essay follows a logical, well-signposted structure (Introduction, Theory, Application, Modifications, Conclusion) with a comprehensive table of contents guiding the reader.
Headings, subheadings and pagination are used consistently to support navigation through complex mathematical content.
weaknesses: Frequent uncompiled LaTeX fragments, truncated figure captions and misaligned tables detract from professional layout.
Key figures lack axis labels and units, and long tables exceed page margins, impeding readability.
Redundant navigation symbols in the contents and occasional formatting inconsistencies reduce overall polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the structural organisation meets the top-band descriptor, inconsistent application of formatting conventions and presentation errors place this work in the lower band for presentation, meriting a score of 2.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The research questionââHow does modelling scoring rates as differential equations compare to the Duckworth-Lewis-Stern method in predicting run chases in rain-affected limited-overs cricket?ââis **explicit, focused and tightly linked** to the ensuing analysis.  Each major section (derivations, applications, modifications, conclusion) is intentionally aligned with addressing this question, and frequent signposting (e.g.
âto evaluate their effectivenessâ¦â) keeps the discussion centred on the RQ.

Although a few peripheral historical notes appear, they do not blur the central investigative line.  The question is **neither too broad nor too narrow** for a 4 000-word EE and provides a robust framework for sustained inquiry, satisfying the highest-band criteria.

The student conducts step-by-step derivations, fits models to data, and computes RÂ² to judge goodness-of-fit.  Comparisons between DLS outputs and model projections are explicitly tabulated, and limitations (over/under-estimation, small sample) are acknowledged.

Yet the **statistical analysis is relatively shallow**: no confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, or error propagation are provided, and conclusions about model superiority rely heavily on RÂ² without deeper scrutiny of residuals or predictive validity.  Consequently, the analysis is **clear and mostly supported** but not fully robust, fitting the 4-6 band with a score of 4.

Mathematical and cricket-specific terminologyâ"exponential growth constant", "carrying capacity", "Wiener process", "par score"âis **largely appropriate and generally accurate**.  However, the essay suffers from **inconsistent symbol use** (e.g.
$R(t)$ sometimes treated as runs, elsewhere as rate), occasional mis-escaped LaTeX, and mismatched variable naming in formulas and narrative.  These lapses disrupt clarity and indicate that terminology, while mostly correct, is **not uniformly precise**, positioning the work in the mid (3-4) band; a score of 3 reflects the frequency of minor errors.

Key layout conventionsâtitle page, word count, page numbers, reference list, tables and figuresâare present and mostly consistent.  However, **frequent LaTeX fragments that fail to compile**, truncated figure captions, and occasional misaligned tables detract from professional presentation.  Some images appear with duplicated alt-text, and long tables exceed page margins.

These issues indicate that while basic formatting requirements are met, application is **inconsistent and occasionally distracting**, placing the work in the 1-2 band with a score of 2.

The student demonstrates **solid understanding of differential calculus and basic stochastic calculus**, deriving exponential and logistic solutions and outlining Itoâs Lemma.  Application to real match data shows generally correct manipulation of mathematical models and calculation of RÂ².

Nevertheless, some derivations contain **notation slips and minor inaccuracies** (e.g.
mis-handled constants, undefined variables in the logistic solution, informal treatment of vertical shifts).  The statistical underpinnings of model choice and validation are treated superficially.  These issues limit the demonstration to a **good but not consistently excellent** level, corresponding to the 3-4 band with a score of 4.

The studentâs topic is **immediately apparent** from the precise title and the opening paragraph, which contextualises the significance of rain-affected cricket and introduces the competing modelling approaches.  The purposeâto compare differential-equation models with the DLS methodâis **clearly articulated** and is revisited in the application, modification and conclusion sections, ensuring a coherent thread throughout the essay.

Explanatory passages on cricket rules, the DLS formula, exponential/logistic growth and stochastic differential equations are **accurate, logically sequenced and pitched at an appropriate mathematical audience**.  Occasional digressions into cricket history do not impede comprehension, and visual aids (tables, graphs) support rather than distract.  Overall, communication of the topic and its mathematical framing is **effective and sustained**, fully meeting the descriptors of the 5-6 band.

The essay exhibits a **logical, well-signposted structure**: title page, table of contents, sequential sections (Introduction â Theory â Application â Modifications â Conclusion) and comprehensive appendices.  Headings, sub-headings and pagination guide the reader effectively, and the flow supports comprehension of increasingly sophisticated mathematics.

Minor redundancies (extended historical background, repeated data tables) marginally affect concision but not overall clarity.  The structure therefore meets the descriptors for the top band (3-4) and earns a score of 4.

Research draws on reputable secondary sources (ESPNcricinfo match data, academic texts on differential and stochastic calculus) and integrates them into the mathematical investigation.  The data are clearly presented in tables, and manual RÂ² calculations exhibit diligence.  However, **only two matches are analysed**, limiting the breadth and generalisability of findings, and some parameter choices (e.g.
vertical shift values) lack empirical grounding.  Thus research is **good and mostly relevant** but falls short of the rigour expected for the top band, meriting a score at the lower end of 4-6.

The discussion integrates mathematical results with cricketing context, explaining why vertical shifts improve fit and why SDEs might address randomness.  The conclusion revisits the RQ and recognises limitations, showing **some critical reflection**.

However, evaluation often remains **qualitative and descriptive**, and alternative interpretations (e.g.
sample bias, overfitting) are only briefly mentioned.  The proposed stochastic extension is theoretical with no empirical test.  The argument is reasoned but **partially critical rather than fully analytical**, aligning with the 4-6 band; a score of 4 is appropriate.

The essay outlines a **complete sequence of methodological steps**: theoretical derivations of exponential and logistic ODEs, parameter estimation via GDC regression, calculation of RÂ², comparison with DLS outputs, and a forward-looking proposal of SDE extensions (with code scaffold).  Primary match data are sourced from ESPNcricinfo and tabulated; parameters, fits and residual calculations are reproduced in an extensive appendix, evidencing **informed selection of tools** (Desmos, Python, manual RÂ²).

However, the empirical base is limited to only two matches, and choices such as the ad-hoc vertical shift are justified only briefly.  The reliance on secondary summaries rather than full ball-by-ball datasets weakens robustness.  Hence, while methodology is largely complete and appropriate, minor gaps in data breadth and parameter justification place it at the lower end of the 5-6 band, meriting a score of 5.

annotations.comment: The solution for the constants in the partial-fraction decomposition is treated informally; a more formal proof of $A=B=1$ would improve precision.
Analysing only two matches limits generalisability and weakens the empirical basis; broader datasets or statistical sampling would strengthen research quality.
Conclusions rely heavily on $R^2$ without examining residual patterns or confidence intervals, reducing depth of statistical evaluation.
The title is precise and immediately communicates the research focus, linking differential-equation modelling to the Duckworth-Lewis-Stern method, establishing a clear investigative purpose.
Including the word count and research question at the start meets IB formatting requirements and provides full context from the outset.
The notation $$ÎC$$ is introduced heuristically; explicitly stating its relation to initial conditions would strengthen mathematical rigor.
The table of contents is clearly structured and comprehensive, enabling the reader to navigate the extended essay effectively.
Symbols $R(t)$ and $S(t)$ are introduced without consistently distinguishing cumulative runs from scoring rate, causing occasional notational ambiguity.
The use of a bullet list without numbering and inconsistent HTML list styling disrupts formal formatting; adopt standard numbered or bulleted list conventions.
The extended historical description of cricket origins, while interesting, digresses from the research question and could be condensed to maintain sharper focus on modelling objectives.
Figure 1 lacks axis titles and units on the graph, which impedes interpretability; each axis should be explicitly labelled for clarity.
The contents table lists redundant navigation controls (ââ Prev Next â¶â) which could be removed to streamline presentation and reduce clutter.
Defining the scoring rate $$S(t)=\frac{dR}{dt}$$ is precise and shows correct application of calculus, underpinning the subsequent differential equation models.
The introduction effectively explains cricketâs basic rules and formats, demonstrating accurate subject-specific terminology and setting a solid foundation for mathematical modelling.
The presentation of the DLS formula $$Z(u,w)=Z_0(w)(1-e^{-b(w)u})$$ is clear and correctly annotated, showing sound knowledge of the underlying exponential decay model.
The resource percentage $$P(u,w)$$ is stated but not derived or justified, limiting deeper understanding of how these empirical values were established.
The overs-by-runs table is clearly formatted, with distinct columns for each team, supporting visual comparison of scoring progress effectively.
Introducing an ad-hoc vertical shift without theoretical justification risks overfitting; a clearer rationale for added parameter $h$ would improve critical evaluation.
The exponential model derivation is thorough, correctly solving $$\frac{dR}{dt}=kR(t)$$ to yield $$R(t)=R_i e^{kt}$$ with clear integration steps.
The logistic differential equation $$\frac{dR}{dt}=rR(t)(1-\frac{R(t)}{K})$$ and its justification are accurately presented, demonstrating solid subject knowledge.
The application section explicitly reintroduces the research question and aligns each match analysis to evaluating model performance, maintaining clear focus.
The summary table of predicted $R(50)$ values neatly consolidates model outputs and $R^2$ metrics, enhancing readability of comparative results.
The logistic model with drift correction is clearly stated as $$R(t)=R_i e^{(k-\tfrac12\sigma^2)t+\sigma W(t)}, demonstrating effective application of Itoâs Lemma.
The repeated alt-text in Figure 3 is redundant and distracts from professional presentation; simplify captions to one clear description.
Figure 4âs image description duplicates earlier formatting errors and lacks a concise caption; refine image labels and ensure consistent styling.
